experiment: freeze/restore throughput audit — fast-path scaling + cascade ordering
_correction_note: An earlier version of this receipt claimed a ~14x 'CRC verify' restore win and ~8x freeze win. That was WRONG: the slow runs wrote/read the snapshot on /workspace (a RunPod network FS with a 20GB quota), the fast runs used /root (local NVMe). Two variables changed at once. The freeze half is provably NOT CRC — freeze never reads THAW_VERIFY (only restore-verify does, pipeline.rs:400/484; freeze CRC at accumulate_freeze_crcs is unconditional). The restore half is confounded with storage. CRC's real cost is UNMEASURED and needs a clean retest (verify on vs off, same local disk). Findings below are the clean ones (single variable changed).

fast_path_scaling.note: pread path (rust_pipelined), THAW_VERIFY unset, snapshot on local NVMe (/root). Per-GPU restore is flat across model size; TP=2 doubles aggregate with no contention on this pod.
fast_path_scaling.headline: a 72B (145GB) model restores from a thaw file in ~5.0s

cascade_ordering.note: Same model (32B), same disk (/root), same verify setting — only the restore path differs. The mmap-first cascade used by restore_model_tp / the TP=1 loader is far slower than O_DIRECT pread for a cold single-GPU restore.

deployment_gotcha: Do NOT put snapshots on a network/quota volume. On /workspace (RunPod network FS) the same 7B restore measured 0.88 GB/s vs 12.56 GB/s on /root (local NVMe). The slow number was the filesystem, not thaw.
shipped_fix: loader.py TP=1 restore cascade now prefers O_DIRECT pread over mmap-first (TP>1 keeps mmap-first for NVMe contention). Justified by cascade_ordering above + theory + prior ordering.
open_questions: CRC verify cost on the restore critical path — unmeasured; retest verify on vs off with the snapshot on the same local NVMe.
mmap-vs-pread ordering is one pod / one NVMe class — re-validate on other storage (slower NVMe, networked) before treating pread-first as universal.
